cases.answer: Proof_T(p,q) checks that p codes a valid T-proof of the formula coded by q: roughly (1) p decodes to a finite sequence of formulas; (2) each line is either an axiom of T or follows from earlier lines by an inference rule; (3) the sequence is well-formed (each entry is a legal formula); and (4) the last line is exactly the formula coded by q. All four checks are bounded, mechanical operations on code-numbers, so Proof_T is primitive recursive — hence decidable, hence representable in T (T proves it of exactly the right (p,q) pairs and refutes it of the rest). Provability is then Prov_T(q) := ∃p Proof_T(p,q): 'some number codes a proof of q'. Adding the unbounded ∃p makes Prov_T only recursively enumerable, not decidable — the crucial asymmetry.

Proof_T(p,q): p decodes to a sequence; every line is an axiom or follows by a rule; all lines are well-formed; the last line is the formula coded by q. These are bounded mechanical checks, so Proof_T is primitive recursive and thus representable in T. Then Prov_T(q):=∃p Proof_T(p,q); the unbounded ∃ makes provability r.e., not decidable.

Proof_T(p,q) verifies p is a real proof of q: it parses p into lines, checks each line is an axiom or a valid inference from earlier ones, and checks the final line is q. Because that's all finite checking, it's primitive recursive and representable in T. Provability is Prov_T(q)=∃p Proof_T(p,q) — there exists a proof — which is only semi-decidable due to the unbounded search.

Proof_T works because the code-numbers understand that they are proofs — the numbers know their own meaning, so Prov_T just asks the number whether it feels provable. Representability means the formula becomes conscious of the proof.
Prov_T(q) is decidable just like Proof_T: there's always an algorithm that halts and tells you whether q is provable, since you just check all the proofs.
The provability predicate is an arithmetic formula that expresses provability. Proof_T checks proofs. This is used to build the Gödel sentence and is very important.
Proof_T is a unit-testing predicate that asserts your proof compiles; Prov_T runs the test suite and returns true if all assertions pass.
